posts.summary: Zed Shaw recounts the exact day he learned to draw — March 10, 2013 — during an intensive drawing course based on Betty Edwards' 'Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain.' He describes bizarre euphoric giggling fits while doing blind contour drawings with his left hand, a sensation no one else he knew had experienced. During a five-day intensive class taught by Edwards' son Brian, Shaw felt a breakthrough on the third day while drawing piers in perspective at Fort Mason. The experience launched an obsession with visual art that led him to teach himself painting and drawing across multiple mediums over the following 19 months, defying the belief instilled in him as a child that nerds aren't creative.
key_points: Shaw experienced inexplicable euphoric giggling while doing drawing exercises, particularly blind contour drawings with his non-dominant left hand
A dramatic breakthrough moment occurred during a five-day intensive drawing course, where effort suddenly gave way to effortless perception
The experience of his right hand 'learning the difference between writing and drawing' illustrates the perceptual shift Edwards' method aims to produce
Shaw's post-breakthrough obsession drove him to master oils, watercolor, acrylic, charcoal, pastel, and digital painting in under two years
The piece directly challenges the cultural assumption that technical/nerdy people lack artistic ability
money_quotes: It sounds like total pseudo-science bullshit, but my right hand had learned the difference between 'writing' and 'drawing', and it wanted to be in charge again.
I felt a doorstop get kicked away from a door I'd been trying to push through.
When I was younger, I was told that I didn't have a single artistic bone in my body. I was a nerd, and nerds do math and aren't creative. Now I know, that's total bullshit.
The sensation is intense, strange, and nobody else I know has this reaction. I asked around, and everyone thinks I'm insane.
I watch more art training videos than I do porn now.
Half-way through the drawing, I calmly, without giggling, but with the euphoria, draw the pier in perspective with measuring and trying to be accurate.
posts.key_insight: Learning to draw is a perceptual breakthrough that can happen at a specific, identifiable moment — and the cultural belief that some people simply aren't artistic is a damaging lie.

posts.summary: Shaw examines how internet social norms protect trolls while punishing their victims, using a Guardian article about an author who tracked down her Goodreads troll as a launching point. He argues that the 'Don't Feed The Trolls' ethos mirrors classic abuser dynamics, giving trolls full license to harass while penalizing any response from victims. He then expands into a broader hierarchy of creative social status, ranking trolls at the top and nerds at the bottom based on what society lets each group get away with. Drawing from his own experience as someone who occupies multiple roles—troll of trolls, artist, author, musician, coder, and nerd—he illustrates how each identity carries different social privileges and penalties. The overall argument is that internet culture has created perverse incentive structures where the most destructive actors enjoy the most protection.
key_points: Internet norms like 'Don't Feed The Trolls' function as an abuser dynamic, giving trolls license to attack while punishing victims who respond
On platforms like Goodreads, authors are forbidden from responding to reviews, making troll reviewers more trusted than the creators themselves
Shaw proposes a creative social hierarchy where trolls sit at the top and nerds at the bottom, based on what behavior society permits each group
Coders occupy a rising but resented position in the hierarchy, drawing hostility from every other creative class despite building the platforms they all use
Being a nerd overrides all other creative identities, coating everything you do with uncoolness regardless of talent or output
money_quotes: The insanity of the internet is not that there are trolls, it's that trolls are given full rights to abuse others and people defend the troll's right to be abusive fucking assholes.
The internet abuser will send out a small pin prick, hoping for a response so they can justify their abuse because the social norms of the internet gives them license to then attack if you respond.
The point of free speech is that everyone gets to talk, not just the first asshole who opens her mouth.
An abuser's entire goal in life is to find a reason to exert their abusive sociopathic desires onto victims while appearing to be in the right.
You'll see nearly every other sector of the creative social class use Twitter, Wordpress, and their iPhones to rant about how coders are destroying the world with their technology.
Being a nerd is at the bottom of the hierarchy because no matter what I do, my nerdiness coats my creative output with the hot sticky air of the uncool.
I tell people I write 'technical books' and they say, 'Oh so not real books.'
posts.key_insight: Internet culture has constructed social norms that grant trolls more protection and social power than any creative class, mirroring the dynamics of real-world abusive relationships where victims are punished for fighting back.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw announces an upcoming series of daily posts exposing problems with coding bootcamps. While acknowledging bootcamps' goal of making coding education accessible, he argues most are exploitative, charging $15k while using his free materials and employing unqualified instructors. He describes receiving reports from students about abuse, sexual harassment, and false effectiveness claims, noting students are afraid to speak out for fear of damaging their careers. He pledges to separate criticism of schools from their students and offers to help bootcamp graduates find work.
key_points: Most bootcamps use freely available materials (including Shaw's own books) while charging students $15k for programs that don't deliver on promises
Students are afraid to criticize their bootcamps because their career prospects are tied to the school's reputation
Some bootcamps employ instructors who cannot actually code and make false claims about graduate employment rates
At least one school has serious allegations of abuse, sexual harassment, and exploitation
Shaw offers to personally help bootcamp graduates find work to offset any damage his exposé might cause
money_quotes: Most just take my books, teach my material, then rip off students for $15k programs that don't actually get anyone deployed.
A lot of students are literally afraid to speak out against these schools for fear that their hard work will be for nothing because they will be associated with the school and not get hired.
It's sad that these students have to put up with abusive and exploitative treatment because their perceived alternative is to be unemployed.
One school is so full of abuse, sexual harassment, and exploitation that I have no idea how they've stayed in business this long.
Many still continue to employ completely unqualified instructors and in some cases, instructors who can't even code.
If they can code it's because they learned despite the school and that nobody can hold the school they attended against them.
I'll take responsibility for possibly ruining your job prospects and do what I can to get you work.
posts.key_insight: Coding bootcamps exploit students' fear of unemployment to charge premium prices for substandard education while silencing criticism through students' dependence on the school's reputation.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that the traditional art world's gatekeeping over what counts as 'art' is hypocritical, since conceptual art already stretches the definition to absurdity. He proposes an alternative definition: art is any activity that induces a state of flow requiring altered brain function. Drawing on neuroscience research about how music engages the whole brain differently, he extends this to programming, writing, painting, and dancing. He suggests flow may explain why people enjoy creative activities as a form of altered consciousness similar to meditation or religious experience. He concludes that art education should focus on teaching students to achieve flow states rather than just producing external results.
key_points: The art world's gatekeeping is hypocritical: if chicken strapped to underwear and garbage on a floor count as art, then finely crafted code qualifies too
Flow—the sensation of timelessness and effortless concentration—is the common thread connecting programming, painting, music, writing, and dancing
Neuroscience suggests creative activities repurpose brain regions for non-standard functions, which may explain both the flow state and its exhausting aftermath
Flow provides an altered consciousness similar to drugs, meditation, or religious experience but with fewer negative consequences
Art education should aim to teach students the skill of achieving flow rather than just evaluating their external output
money_quotes: Sorry nerd, you don't have neck tattoos and heroin track marks so you can't possibly be doing art.
Basically, if you want to call what you do art, then it's art. If anyone tries to tell you it's not, then they're not artists.
This intense feeling of concentration where time stops and everything in my body and mind works seamlessly as if my self doesn't exist.
It could be that flow is simply the ability to make the parts of your brain do something different for a little while. A kind of vacation for your hippocampus.

What makes coding an art is that it requires making your brain do something it's not normally designed to do which then causes a sensation similar to meditation and requires effort but feels effortless.
Instead of having to sit quietly for hours praying or meditating you can do some art, read, write, or code.
If the student is able to do this little mental trick then they'll get much more enjoyment out of the activity than just what they produce.
The best art causes this flow in others, and if you've ever seen someone play a video game or browse the internet for days on end, then you know programming beats everything in that department.
posts.key_insight: Art should be defined not by what it produces but by the flow state it induces in the creator—a neurological altered consciousness that programming, painting, music, and writing all share.

posts.summary: Shaw proposes a detailed consumer privacy law that would require companies to disclose which employees can access user accounts, maintain visible access logs, and let users block specific employees from viewing their data. The proposal includes mandatory credential-access disclosures at login screens, monthly access activity notifications, and requirements that company agents identify themselves when interacting with users. Shaw acknowledges the proposal is technically feasible but politically impossible, as companies have no incentive to reveal their internal access practices. He concludes with cynical realism that even if consumers had this information, most wouldn't act on it.
key_points: Login screens should prominently display which companies' employees can view your credentials and account data, not buried in privacy policies
Companies should provide access logs showing the name, company, and reason for every employee who accessed a user's account
Account holders should be able to explicitly block named employees from accessing their accounts, with fines for violations
Company employees interacting with users must be identified as agents of the company to prevent covert manipulation
The proposal is technically trivial but politically impossible because companies profit from unrestricted internal access to user data
money_quotes: I remember one company I worked at had an employee who routinely scanned people's files looking for juicy bits of information he could view.
This means that you now know that an employee at Google has the ability to look up your Wifi password, drive a car near your house, and log into your network to packet sniff your data.
They always allow their employees unrestricted access until they get their ass sued off over it.
If privacy is important, then it stands to reason that knowing who is potentially violating it is an important part of managing your personal security.
There's nothing radical or onerous about what's proposed. It's actually just good security practice at any company to restrict access to accounts.
Privacy and security have been so fully eroded in internet culture that even when given this information consumers wouldn't care.
If a law like this were even proposed you would see a cash tsunami rain down on Washington DC like it was a Thai fishing village.
posts.key_insight: The basic security practice of logging and disclosing who accesses user accounts is technically trivial but will never be implemented because companies profit from the opacity.

posts.summary: Shaw recounts a plein air painting session at Baker Beach in San Francisco, where he painted a small oil study before the rising tide forced him to leave. He describes his compact 'cutie kit' setup that allows him to hold a palette and paint while standing. He critiques his own work, noting the water came out too green and lacked reflected sky light. The post focuses on practical adjustments he's making to his portable painting kit, including reducing brushes and limiting his palette to four colors for faster setup in challenging outdoor conditions.
key_points: Shaw uses a compact thumb-hole painting kit that lets him paint standing up in outdoor locations
He completed a small painting in about 30 minutes before the tide reached his shoes
He self-critiques the painting's color problems, specifically that the water looks like grass due to insufficient reflected sky light
He streamlined his kit to just four paint tubes (Yellow Ochre, Napthol Scarlet, Chromatic Black, Titanium White) for easier portability
He plans to pre-mix and lay out colors on the palette before going out to save setup time in the field
money_quotes: Yes, all you people living in colder places are so much tougher than anyone living in San Francisco. Sure. Totally.
I managed to get this painting down in about 30 minutes then the tide was hitting my shoes so I bailed.
I ended up making the water way too green so it looks like a weird field of grass rather than water.
There would need to be more reflected sky light in the water to make it look less like grass.
It's kind of a pain to be standing there in the cold watching the tide and trying to lay out blobs of paint before you can paint.
These days I carry this little case around with me all the time so I can do some tiny oil paintings whenever I want.
posts.key_insight: Constraints like weather, tides, and portability drive practical innovations in a plein air painting practice, pushing toward simpler setups and more efficient workflows.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw announces he's removing his 'Deconstructing K&R C' section from Learn C the Hard Way after years of backlash from the C programming community. Despite his goal of teaching people to critically analyze code—even code written by famous programmers—he was met with hostility from pedants who admitted he was technically correct but refused to accept his critique of their heroes. He reframes the book as a course in writing secure C code and rigorous programming analysis, while subtly encouraging readers to move on to newer languages like Go, Rust, and Swift. Shaw views the episode as emblematic of a toxic programming culture that prioritizes hero worship and trivia memorization over genuine education and improvement.
key_points: Shaw's critique of K&R C code was technically correct but drew intense backlash because it challenged revered figures in programming
The C programming community's response exemplifies a culture where questioning established authorities is treated as heresy regardless of factual accuracy
Experienced programmers who haven't written professional C code in years still gatekeep the language through standards memorization and pedantry
Shaw is pivoting the book toward teaching secure C coding and rigorous code analysis as transferable skills, while steering readers toward modern alternatives
He declares C effectively dead for new programmers, viewing Go, Rust, and Swift as superior replacements for most use cases
money_quotes: I cannot fathom how a group of people who are supposedly so intelligent and geared toward rational thought can hold in their head the idea that I can be wrong, and also right at the same time.
Frankly, this is the deep dark ugly evil side of programming culture.

They talk all day long of how, 'We're all in this together' but if you don't bow to the great altar of their vast knowledge and beg them for permission to question what they believe you are suddenly the enemy.
Programmers consistently go out of their way to set themselves up in positions of power that require others to pay homage to their brilliant ability to memorize standards or know obscure trivia.
I cannot help old programmers. They are all doomed.

I'd rather make the book useful for people who can learn C and how to make it solid than fight a bunch of closed minded conservatives who's only joy in life is feeling like they know more about a pedantic pathetically small topic like C undefined behavior.
C's dead. It's the language for old programmers who want to debate section A.6.2 paragraph 4 of the undefined behavior of pointers. Good riddance.
posts.key_insight: Programming culture's hero worship and gatekeeping through trivia memorization actively prevents honest critique of established code and harms efforts to teach newcomers how to write safer software.

posts.summary: Shaw draws a satirical parallel between artists and entrepreneurs, arguing both groups occupy inflated positions of status in society while primarily serving the ultra-wealthy. He mocks the idea from The Atlantic that 'entrepreneurs are the new artists' by showing how both groups share traits of self-importance, entitlement, and a belief they deserve free money simply for existing. Through sections on sexiness, world-changing ambitions, entitlement, and importance, he argues both groups produce work that ultimately benefits wealthy patrons while claiming to serve society. He criticizes Molly Crabapple's call for universal basic income as self-serving artist entitlement rather than genuine concern for the poor. The piece concludes that both artists and entrepreneurs have been granted undeserved social status for 'doing so little.'
key_points: Both artists and entrepreneurs primarily serve the ultra-wealthy while claiming to change the world for everyone
Artist entitlement and entrepreneur entitlement are structurally identical — both demand free money for simply existing and declaring their work important
The art market and the startup ecosystem both function as speculation vehicles for the wealthy, not as engines of social good
Society grants both groups mythological status that is wildly disproportionate to their actual contributions
Universal basic income advocacy from artists is self-serving when framed as benefiting art rather than benefiting the poor
money_quotes: Entrepreneurs and artists now share that incredible sweet spot in our society of being given positions of great status for doing so little.
They are allowed to produce anything and declare it a sign that they are somehow more special than the rest of us.
That's how you change the world when you're an artist. You soften the hearts of the wealthy while they're banging a stripper at their Nazi themed sex parties.
Entrepreneurs are the good looking, strong willed, powerful future despots of the Kingdom of Corporate and everyone else is just a worthless nobody who needs to work for them.
I still don't know why artists think they shouldn't have to work like everyone else to gain the freedom to do what they love.
Once they get that money their entire life's goal is to then avoid working at all costs.
No longer should they have to struggle to do their important work of making things for the ultra wealthy.
posts.key_insight: Artists and entrepreneurs are mirror images of the same entitlement — both claim world-changing importance while functioning as status-seeking servants of the ultra-wealthy.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that 'Voyeur Marketing' — the practice of using personal data from social networks to target ads — is fundamentally flawed because knowing someone's private life doesn't predict purchases as well as knowing their past purchases. He contends that the distance between a life event and an actual purchase is mediated by research and brand loyalty, making personal data statistically invalid as a purchase predictor. He contrasts Amazon's effective recommendation engine (based on purchase history) with Twitter and Facebook's ineffective ad targeting (based on social behavior). Shaw predicts social networks will eventually pivot to building direct marketplaces to compete with Amazon, closing the gap between social activity and actual transactions.
key_points: Past purchase behavior is the only statistically valid predictor of future purchases, not personal or social data
Voyeur marketing is an inferior product competing against purchase-history-based targeting from companies like Amazon
The gap between a life event and a purchase is filled by research and brand loyalty, creating confounding variables that invalidate personal data as predictors
Social networks will eventually build direct marketplaces to close the distance between their users and actual transactions
Google Adwords succeeds not through surveillance but through keyword-purchase matching combined with past search behavior
money_quotes: Voyeur Marketing is an attempt to subvert the market by being the first one to offer a consumer some products so they don't see your competitors'.
The only statistically valid indicator of future purchases is past purchases.
It's almost a kind of first cousin, twice removed, blackmail scheme.
Twitter is so desperate to make their ads effective they're force following celebrities like William Shatner onto Mastercard.
My behavior with my friends is not an indicator of whether I want to buy more lube today. Sorry Google.
Voyeur marketing will be inferior as long as there's companies who have access to the things I really bought.
The fallacy of building a large user base just to sell ads to marketers doesn't work because the behavior of purchases is too far removed from the products on most social networks.
posts.key_insight: Surveillance-based advertising is fundamentally outcompeted by purchase-history targeting because private life events are poor predictors of buying behavior compared to what someone actually bought before.

posts.summary: Shaw writes an open letter to Slack proposing a simple feature: a paid membership checkbox for Slack teams. He wants to use Slack as a platform to teach coding, where students would pay to join his Slack team, with Slack handling all payment processing and taking a cut. He argues Slack already has every feature needed except the ability to invert who pays, and frames it as a mutual business opportunity that would save him from dealing with payment and membership management overhead.
key_points: Slack could become a paid community platform with one simple feature: charging members to join a team
Content creators shouldn't have to build payment infrastructure when platforms already have it
The proposed model benefits both parties: creator provides content and promotion, platform handles payments and takes a cut
Shaw identifies a gap between Slack's existing B2B model and potential creator-economy use cases
money_quotes: All I want is a checkbox when I create my 'team' that reads: 'Members pay $_____ to join.'
I'd rather just hang out in the Slack and answer questions and have them pay you so you pay me.
Just need the ability to invert who pays and it's nearly perfect.
You literally have every single feature I want to make this work. I just don't want to deal with the bullshit of payments and invites and booting people who don't pay and all that crap.
I have the clout and the skills. You have the platform.
posts.key_insight: Platforms like Slack sit on untapped creator-economy potential that could be unlocked by simply inverting their payment model to let members pay creators through the platform.

posts.summary: Shaw argues against Pixar's 'plussing' culture — the idea that you should never criticize but only build on others' ideas. He contends that criticism is essential to producing quality work in any creative field, whether art, code, or writing. He points to Pixar's wage theft scandal as evidence that 'and then' cultures are really passive-aggressive power structures that suppress dissent. Shaw advocates a balanced approach: create freely in isolation first, then apply rigorous self-criticism before presenting work to teams. He rejects both toxic positivity and abusive criticism, arguing that honest feedback is what separates professionals from delusional amateurs.
key_points: The 'plussing' philosophy from improv comedy, when applied to workplaces, becomes a passive-aggressive power play that suppresses legitimate criticism
Pixar's wage theft scandal reveals the dark side of 'never criticize' cultures — they breed leaders who exploit employees who've been conditioned not to push back
Quality work in any discipline requires a cycle of uninhibited creation followed by ruthless self-criticism and willingness to throw things out
Programmers and artists both use personal investment as a shield against criticism, claiming their work is a 'labor of love' to deflect valid complaints
The best creative process starts with individual ideation, then uses teams for production and refinement — not group brainstorming
money_quotes: Negativity and criticism are an important part of creating anything of quality. You have to be critical of what you make or else you end up making total crap and never improve.
Taking criticism in stride is just part of quality, and trying to eliminate any criticism of your ideas is a sign of both immaturity and abuse of power.
The word we use for an artist who believes everything they make is fantastic is 'delusional'.

Programmers are notorious for building crystal palaces crafted of finely tuned deeply complex structures that follow all the rules, binding together every meta-pattern possible, and they end up with a total shit show of a project.
Any time you see unrealistic claims of utopia you can just bet its hiding something sinister.
Nobody buying your shit is an Andthener.
If you actually give a shit about being good at something then you'll know that people are blowing smoke up your ass and find it incredibly annoying.
Calling criticism 'abuse' is the belief of a child. Adults understand the difference between 'I don't think this will work' and 'I think you're a fucking piece of shit.'
posts.key_insight: Cultures that ban criticism don't eliminate negativity — they just drive it underground where it becomes manipulation, power abuse, and exploitation by those at the top.

posts.summary: Shaw introduces the concept of 'dork distance' — the unavoidable period of public incompetence that beginners must endure to develop any skill. He argues that mastery in painting, music, programming, or any practiced skill requires accumulating 'mileage' (miles of canvas, hours of practice, pages of code), and that this distance must be traveled while looking foolish. He uses his own unabashed dorkiness as proof that social embarrassment is survivable and even liberating. His practical advice: post your beginner attempts online under your real name or a pseudonym, endure the initial panic, and discover that the fear was imagined. The post is both a personal manifesto on embracing awkwardness and an educational philosophy about how fear of embarrassment is the primary barrier to learning.
key_points: Mastery requires 'mileage' — a measurable distance of practice (miles of canvas, hours of playing, pages of code) that no shortcut can replace
Beginners are inherently dorky because they don't yet know the social norms of their chosen activity, making embarrassment unavoidable
Fear of looking foolish is the primary barrier to learning, not lack of talent or intelligence
Posting your beginner work publicly is the fastest way to overcome dork fear because the imagined consequences rarely materialize
Men who enforce 'cool' social conformity are the real problem, not the people willing to fail openly
money_quotes: I can't let feeling like a dork get in the way of progress in the things I love.
A great many skills are of the kind that have very simple concepts which can be learned in about a week, but then require years of constant training and effort to get better.
Getting good involves values and mileage.
If it's something that you need to practice, then there's some kind of distance you have to travel to get good at it.
You have to go this distance as a dork because as a beginner you are clueless of social norms in that activity.
Fear of being a dork will limit you in your future educational goals, but it's a fear that's easily overcome.
The internet's not real. You'll post your total turd of a painting, song, code, and then nothing will happen.
You start getting good and stop being a dork.
posts.key_insight: The unavoidable awkwardness of being a beginner — the 'dork distance' — is the real barrier to mastery, and the fastest way past it is to post your failures publicly until the fear loses its power.

posts.summary: Shaw describes his evolution in how he uses painting instructional videos, moving from passive note-taking to actively painting along. After experiences with Bob Ross, Johannes Vloothius, and Richard Robinson — including an in-person workshop — he had the obvious realization that following along with paint in hand is far more effective than watching and taking notes. He argues this approach lets him always have a subject to paint and combines practice sessions with learning. The post concludes with ten practical tips for anyone wanting to paint along with instructional videos.
key_points: Passive video watching and note-taking is far less effective than actively painting along with the instructor
Following along with videos provides an always-available painting subject, eliminating the excuse of having nothing compelling to paint
The ability to pause and rewind gives video-based learning an advantage over live workshops and classes
Beginners need foundational skills before attempting to follow along, or the experience becomes frustrating rather than educational
The biggest mistake in both painting and coding is thinking that adding more will fix what's broken — sometimes you need to scrape it off and start over
money_quotes: Why the fuck am I just watching this?! I have paint and supplies right here. I could follow along!
I learned way more from following along with Richard than I ever did just watching and taking notes.
The biggest mistake beginners in painting and coding make is thinking that adding more paint/code more will fix broken paint/code. It doesn't. Just scrape it off and try again.
He's better than a pile of Ambien for going to sleep.
The video is more of a guide than an exact instruction set.
Now I always have a subject to paint because if I have my laptop and my paintbox I can startup a video and paint along with any number of artists.
posts.key_insight: Active practice alongside instruction — not passive consumption and note-taking — is the most effective way to learn a craft skill.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw writes an open letter to Paul Graham criticizing Hacker News as a platform that enables sustained harassment and abuse while YCombinator profits from it. He recounts seven years of repeated slander through anonymous accounts and recycled attack posts, arguing that YC selectively moderates only criticism of its own startups while allowing personal attacks on others. Shaw positions himself as someone with nothing to lose who could retaliate by publicly reviewing and criticizing YC startups, leveraging his large audience of coding students. He ultimately appeals to Graham to either shut down HN comments or heavily moderate them, arguing the experiment has run its course.
key_points: Hacker News enables sustained harassment through anonymous throwaway accounts while YCombinator selectively protects only its own startups from criticism
Shaw argues his lack of VC ties and financial ambition makes him uniquely positioned to fight back since he has nothing to lose
The YC/HN relationship is compared to an abortion clinic that secretly feeds the protestors harassing its own patrons
Shaw details how seven years of HN harassment forced him to hide personal relationships and warn employers about his online reputation
He threatens to use his audience of millions of coding students to actively steer people away from YC startups as retaliation
money_quotes: You saved the business of technology from the useless MBAs who did nothing but drive businesses into the ground on the backs of programmers like me.
I learned a long time ago that you never fight an ugly man because he's got nothing to lose. In the world of startups I'm one ugly motherfucker.
I don't play the game at all and that means I'm unclean by VC standards.
Your news site is nothing more than a slander factory Paul, and you allow YC to back it which makes you responsible.
Because of HN I never mention when I'm out with a loved one. I tell anyone who's friends with me to stay away from my twitter account.
Nobody should have to live like that, and while I don't mind too much, I wonder if there's people out there who have had their lives destroyed by HN commenters and have to hide.
I like to say 'fuck' and stand up for the little guy so that means, no matter how good of a person I am, I will never get respect from people with money.
One of the reasons most of your moronic baby penis followers hate me is because I wiped out epic amounts of Ruby on Rails with one blog post full of truth.
posts.key_insight: Online platforms run by corporations with financial interests will always selectively moderate to protect their own investments while allowing unchecked abuse of outsiders who lack institutional power.

posts.summary: Shaw recounts his experience learning digital painting during a six-hour flight, comparing it to traditional painting. He argues that digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand color mixing by presenting infinite color pickers instead of simulating how painters actually combine pigments from limited palettes. Despite this major flaw, he finds digital painting useful for portability and cost, and describes his process of following along with Richard Robinson's painting videos using ArtRage on a cheap graphics tablet. He draws a parallel between his painting learning method and his coding education philosophy: forcing yourself to work from memory rather than side-by-side copying.
key_points: Digital painting tools fail at color mixing because they present infinite color pickers instead of simulating how painters actually combine limited primary colors
The learning method of switching between full-screen reference and full-screen workspace forces visual memory training, paralleling Shaw's approach to teaching coding
Digital painting's main advantages are portability, lower cost, and safety compared to traditional media with solvents and pigments
Pre-mixing and storing colors in a samples palette proved more effective than trying to mix colors on the fly in digital tools
Following along with painting videos that include the instructor's mistakes is valuable because you learn to fix errors alongside them
money_quotes: Most of the painting programs are in that weird uncanny valley where they're close, but so irritatingly far that they feel awkward and weird.
They seem to think 'mixing' means choosing a color from all the possible colors. That's not how a painter works. I make new colors by combining colors from two piles of paint.
My gamut is actually fairly small, which actually makes mixing paint color easier because I can narrow my palette of primary colors down.
Putting down strokes of color is not the difficult part. It's getting the color right that's hard.
Don't put them side-by-side as that doesn't exercise your ability to remember blocks of code. You'll learn faster if you have to cover what you're typing in while you type it.

No solvents, water, paint, paper, canvases, brushes, tubes of paint, paper towels, or any of the crap. Hell, it just takes all the fun out of getting into painting anyway. No more toys to buy. I should put this in the disadvantages column actually now that I think about it.
I also end up copying Richard's mistakes and then learning how to fix them, which is really helpful.
posts.key_insight: Digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand painters by offering infinite color selection instead of simulating the constrained, subtractive process of mixing from a limited palette of primaries.

posts.summary: Shaw proposes a framework for categorizing paintings along two axes: liberal vs. conservative drawing, and liberal vs. conservative painting (color/application). He observes that American Impressionism typically features conservative (accurate) drawing with liberal (sloppy) painting, which he sees as a safe cliché. He maps out four quadrants — from hyperrealism (conservative/conservative) to abstract expressionism (liberal/liberal) — and plans a systematic painting project exploring all four dimensions using the same subject across four canvases.
key_points: American Impressionism can be characterized as 'correctly drawn, sloppily painted' — a safe formula that lets artists prove technical skill while appearing expressive
Paintings can be categorized on two independent axes: how liberally or conservatively the drawing is done, and how liberally or conservatively the paint is applied

Each quadrant maps to recognizable art movements: hyperrealism (conservative/conservative), cubism (liberal/conservative), American Impressionism (conservative/liberal), abstract expressionism (liberal/liberal)
Shaw plans a methodical project painting the same subject four times, once in each quadrant, to explore how changing one dimension at a time affects the result
money_quotes: What Americans seem to like is a form of impressionism that is 'correctly drawn, sloppily painted'.

He took a kleenex and flopped it around on the canvas to fuck it up. It was if he couldn't bring himself to paint it sloppy but knew that a sloppy painting with correct proportions and perspective would sell well.
It's a safe cliché that lets you be conservative and prove you can paint correctly, but you just decide not to because you're totally a liberal artist libertine type dude.
I've experienced where if I just paint at random and pretend I'm letting go that people seem to think I'm crafting genius when it's just random bullshit.
I think it was Jackson Pollock who said you don't walk into a fucking rose garden and ask what it means. You just go in there because it's pretty and you like it.

It's yet another cliché to slap paint on a canvas randomly and then declare it a statement about the plight of the poor while you sell it for hundreds of thousands of dollars to rich asshole who will lock it in his million dollar apartment.
If you get the values and drawing right you can do anything with color.
posts.key_insight: The tension between technical accuracy and expressive freedom in painting mirrors a broader cultural dynamic, and systematically exploring both axes can reveal which combinations produce genuinely interesting art versus comfortable clichés.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that the tech education world fails to distinguish between people who have never coded at all and those with a few months of experience, lumping both into 'beginner.' He proposes adopting terminology from art education: 'beginning coders' for people with zero experience, and 'early coders' for those who grasp syntax but struggle with problem-solving. Beginning coders need mechanical repetition to internalize concrete skills like syntax and typing, while early coders need guided projects to develop abstract problem-solving. Shaw attributes this blind spot to programmers' inability to visualize someone with zero knowledge, which he sees as a root cause of poor programming education.
key_points: Most tech educators define 'beginner' as someone with 4-6 months of experience, completely ignoring people with zero coding knowledge
Programmers who advocate teaching programming without teaching coding are revising their own learning history — they learned by typing code from books, not from abstract courses
Beginning coders need concrete, repetitive skill-building (like learning scales in music), while early coders need guided projects to develop abstract problem-solving
Shaw's method uses four programming languages to move someone from beginning to early stage, finding that cross-language exposure helps students grasp that syntax represents abstract concepts
The inability to visualize a total novice is a symptom of most programmers being terrible at teaching programming
money_quotes: After 6 months you can't really say someone is a beginner since, well, 6 months later is not the beginning.
This showed a completely ignored group of people, and it's a very good sign that most technologists simply have no concept that there are non-programmers who want to learn programming.
This inability to visualize a person who is a total beginner is a symptom of most programmers being terrible at teaching programming.
A beginning coder doesn't know how to type the | (pipe) character. They don't even know it's called a 'pipe'. I'm not joking about this.
I don't know anyone who took an abstract 'no coding' class and then went and wrote Java or Lisp without going through at least one book teaching how to code.
It's this traversing of abstraction and concrete implementation that I believe takes someone past the early stage and into the junior programmer world.
posts.key_insight: The tech world's failure to distinguish between someone with zero coding experience and someone with a few months of practice leads to educational materials that serve neither group well.

posts.summary: Shaw describes his seemingly supernatural ability to trigger software bugs in every system he touches, from Pearson's royalty payment system to Google Kubernetes to his bank's internal databases. He recounts a pattern spanning decades where routine actions like changing an address or adding bold text cause mysterious failures that support teams can't explain. He argues this curse actually made him a better programmer, since he learned to write defensively by assuming everything would break. He also criticizes companies for expecting users to file detailed bug reports and for getting hostile when bugs are pointed out.
key_points: Shaw triggers obscure edge-case bugs with routine actions across virtually every platform he uses, from financial systems to developer tools
Companies react poorly to bug reports—Paypal/eBay employees harassed him for pointing out a double-login vulnerability that could enable phishing
He argues bug reporters shouldn't have to navigate complex tracking systems; the company should fill out the ticket and ask the reporter to verify it
His constant exposure to broken software made him a better developer who writes defensively by default
Even developer-oriented tools like Kubernetes failed him repeatedly despite following official documentation exactly
money_quotes: You want to find out if your software works well? Have me try to use it.
I'm just cursed with a weird devil hand that drives software insane is all.
I went from saying Paypal is broken to saying they're fucking terrible programmers.

Oh, and I don't fucking work for you. I'm not going to waste 15-30 minutes navigating your bullshit bug tracker, filling out every random Jira/Bugzilla/Confluence/AgileXPHardon12000 tracking data point just so you can ignore me.
You fill out the damn ticket and ask me if it's right. That's what I do. I don't make other people fill out bug reports. That's just rude.
I'm like the coder from Salusa Secondus who had no idea things weren't supposed to be this broken and ended up just making things solid to avoid it.
I've brought systems to their knees just casually clicking around.
posts.key_insight: Constantly encountering software bugs as a user breeds defensive programming habits, but the tech industry's hostility toward bug reporters discourages the very feedback it needs most.

posts.summary: Shaw uses an extended satirical dialogue to criticize open source software projects that cater to difficult community members at the expense of regular users. The allegory portrays FLOSS maintainers as bakers who put 'turds' (bad design decisions) in free cookies to appease a loud, obnoxious 'sentient fly' (toxic power users), while charging others for the clean version. The piece skewers the defensive posture maintainers take when receiving criticism, hiding behind the 'it's free' defense and bureaucratic contribution processes. Shaw argues that offering something for free doesn't immunize it from criticism, and that prioritizing toxic community members over ordinary users is self-destructive. The story ends with the café empty except for the fly—illustrating how this dynamic drives away the broader user base.
key_points: Open source projects often make design concessions to appease loud, toxic community members rather than serving the majority of users
The 'it was free' defense is used to deflect legitimate criticism of poor software decisions
Contribution processes (bug trackers, forms, codes of conduct) are weaponized to silence critics rather than improve the product
Maintainers conflate worship and gratitude with genuine user feedback, prioritizing ego over quality
The end result of catering to toxic minorities is an empty restaurant—a project that drove away its actual user base
money_quotes: You put free cookies out with turds in them because a sentient fly demands turds, and then you charge people for cookies without turds? That's fucking insane.
You offered them to me asshole! If I'd known there was turds in the free cookies I would have just bought the fucking turdless cookies from you.
No, that's destructive because it is telling me to change my mind about a clearly obvious and brilliant decision I made to put turds in my cookies.
All patrons must submit orders through our RTFMS and we handle only the orders we feel are worth our time.
If I give you free cookies you have to worship me! Worship me for my free cookies asshole!
The truth comes out! I have to worship you and it's not about having the best cookies in town. It's about you being recognized by giant sentient flies and nobody else.
Probably because you suck so bad nobody would pay you to do it for real.
posts.key_insight: Open source projects that prioritize appeasing their loudest toxic users over serving the broader community end up with an empty room and a product nobody else wants to use.

posts.summary: Shaw describes his teaching method of having beginners write comments first, then fill in code underneath—a technique he calls converting complex problems into 'paint-by-numbers' problems. He applies this same principle across disciplines: coding, writing, and painting all benefit from outlining before executing. He then proposes a code editor that would automate this process, using machine learning to suggest code beneath comments while continuously running tests. The editor would essentially formalize what programmers already do—searching Stack Overflow and documentation—into an integrated, intelligent workflow. Written in 2015, the idea presciently describes something close to modern AI coding assistants.
key_points: Beginners know what they want code to do but can't bridge the gap to implementation, so starting with English descriptions (comments) gives them a foothold
The comment-first, fill-in-the-blanks approach is universal across creative disciplines—coding, writing, and painting all benefit from outlining before executing
Solving complex problems in any medium means converting them into paint-by-numbers problems: breaking the whole into tiny solvable pieces
A code editor could use machine learning and curated code catalogs to auto-suggest implementations beneath comments while continuously running tests
This would formalize what programmers already do informally—searching Stack Overflow and docs using the same words they'd put in a comment
money_quotes: They don't have enough experience to close the gap, but since they can describe what they want the function to do then that's their start.
I find that starting with desired results works best for beginners and early coders.
In general, the way you can solve a complex problem that's difficult to visualize in any medium (code, words, paint, music, etc.) is to convert the problem to a paint-by-numbers problem.
Instead of just trying to do it all at once right in your head and get it right, you break it down into tiny problems, then solve each one.
Why not have the editor use fancy machine learning algorithms and a vast catalog of existing curated code to do this for you?
Just a random idea, but could be an interesting thing to research. Call it 'Comment Driven Coding' for lack of a better name.
posts.key_insight: Complex creative problems in any medium—code, writing, painting—are best solved by decomposing them into small, describable pieces and filling in the blanks, a process that machines could eventually automate.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that creators across poetry, art, and open source software use personal tragedy as a shield against legitimate criticism of their work. He observes this pattern in cafe poetry readings, abstract art installations, and open source projects, where attaching emotional meaning to mediocre work makes it immune to honest feedback. Shaw contends this is unfair to audiences and users, who deserve the right to genuine reactions. He advocates for accepting criticism when you publish work publicly, handling abusive people separately, and taking responsibility when your work has real problems.
key_points: Attaching personal emotional significance to creative work creates an unfair shield against legitimate criticism
Abstract and open-ended works are especially vulnerable to this exploit because artists can retroactively assign deep meaning to random output
Open source developers use the same defense—claiming their project is a 'labor of love' to deflect bug reports and criticism
Publishing work publicly means accepting that people will react to it, including negatively
The correct approach is to fight actual assholes on their merits, listen to everyone else, and apologize when you genuinely screw up
money_quotes: By attaching a personal emotional connection to what he's created he has shielded himself from criticism.
Because abstract creative works are open to interpretation, an artist can crank out total randomness and then back into the deep personal meaning to shield it from criticism and sell it.
This is unfair to the audience because it removes our power to react to the art in a genuine way, even if that reaction is, 'Fuck that sucks.'
If you don't want that then don't put your shit out there. Go find a little group that will keep it quiet until you can handle it.
The only hardship that inspires my software is another project sucking so bad I craft something better in a fit of rage.
You can't go around saying that simply having invested your time in it means that nobody can get pissed at you for writing buggy shitty code.
But if someone finds a bug in your shit, and it ruins their fucking day, then it's your fault and you should apologize and fucking fix it.
What kind of an asshole uses a kid with cancer to avoid fixing a fucking bug?
posts.key_insight: Using personal tragedy to shield your public work from criticism is a manipulative tactic that robs audiences and users of their right to honest response, whether the work is poetry, art, or open source software.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that meritocracy, while appealing in theory as a way to judge people solely on skill, fails catastrophically in practice because it creates 'Mr. Teflon' — toxic employees who leverage one past achievement to become untouchable despite terrible behavior. He contrasts programming and painting as genuinely skill-based activities where competence is easy to verify, then shows how the meritocracy ideal gets weaponized to protect incompetent assholes. Shaw acknowledges the alternative — bureaucratic HR-driven organizations — has its own problems of credentialism, but at least provides mechanisms to remove toxic people. He concludes that self-employment is the only true meritocracy.
key_points: Meritocracy's original appeal was letting weirdos and outsiders succeed based on skill alone, regardless of background or appearance
In practice, meritocracy creates 'Mr. Teflon' — people who did one impressive thing and become permanently unfireable despite toxic, abusive, or criminal behavior
The inverse of meritocracy — credential-obsessed bureaucracies — is equally abusive but rewards socioeconomic background instead of skill
HR-driven organizations, despite their downsides, at least have procedures to remove toxic employees that meritocracies lack
A functional workplace requires a social contract: everyone tones down their personal extremes so the environment stays professional
money_quotes: You can either write software or you can't. I'm not saying that people who can are special. Hell, total absolute complete fucking idiots with dried husks for skulls write code.
These pretty motherfuckers get away with literal murder while a weirdo like me gets death threats because I don't like Haskell.
Everyone who claims they have a meritocracy then uses this to act like total fucking assholes because if you extend the concept of meritocracy too far you can excuse any obnoxious ass behavior.
The failure of a meritocracy is that it has become a way to abuse people.

Originally it was so that people who were different could keep their jobs in the face of mediocre losers who felt everyone should be just like them to have a job. Now it's used by mediocre losers to keep other mediocre losers in jobs just because they're all alike.
The inverse of meritocracy is just as abusive and rewards people for their socioeconomic backgrounds rather than giving them a chance to shine despite where they come from.
Actually, I'll just work for myself since that's the ultimate meritocracy.
posts.key_insight: Meritocracy in tech has been corrupted from a system that protects talented outsiders into one that shields toxic employees from consequences, making it just another form of workplace abuse.

posts.summary: Shaw introduces the concept of 'Educational Mithridatism' — deliberately building tolerance for boring but necessary practice, named after the king who immunized himself against poison. He argues that hating repetitive exercises like scales or flashcards is completely normal, not a character flaw, and rejects both the puritanical worship of mindless work and the bohemian rejection of rote learning. The ideal approach combines rote training for foundational skills with creative application. Shaw provides a concrete method: establish a baseline tolerance, incrementally increase exposure time, take breaks, and periodically test whether the practice is paying off. The core message is that the ability to endure productive boredom is a trainable skill, not an innate virtue.
key_points: Hating boring repetitive practice is universal and normal — people who tolerate it aren't morally superior, just differently wired or trained
The belief that grinding repetition builds character stems from America's puritanical history, while bohemians overcorrect by rejecting all rote learning
The optimal educational approach combines rote memorization of building blocks with creative application of those skills
Tolerance for productive boredom can be systematically trained through incremental exposure, like building immunity to poison
Shaw's books use exercises like flashcards and repetitive commands specifically because rote memorization removes the symbol-recognition bottleneck from learning programming
money_quotes: Some things that you need to do are a lot like arsenic. These are activities that you know you should do, but the act of doing them simply drives you mad and feels like they are killing you.
Many people who react this way to practice end up only ever attempting the things they can do naturally, which is really not many things for most humans.

It's a myth that there's a small percentage of people who just can't sit still and the rest of the world are perfect little angels who can do exactly as told and mindlessly write their names a million times to learn penmanship.
People who like doing boring shit are not magically better moral individuals than you are.
What you want is to be neither of those people by being both of them at the same time.
The world of programming is loaded with people who have memorized every square inch of manuals and standards, but who couldn't code their way out of a lego castle.
Admit that you hate it, that it feels like poison, it's going to kill you, and take the challenge and build your tolerance.
You aren't a less moral or stupid person because you can't focus. You're just someone who never learned how to do it and need to train yourself.
posts.key_insight: The ability to endure necessary but boring practice is not an innate character trait but a trainable skill, and framing it as poison you can build immunity to removes the shame and provides a concrete path forward.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw describes his experience with someone impersonating him on Facebook and Facebook's refusal to take action. He was forced to create his own Facebook account just to report the impostor, which he argues constitutes extortion of signups. Facebook's response was bureaucratic deflection rather than resolution. He argues that Facebook's claim to enforce real-name identity policies while failing to remove impostors makes them liable for any resulting slander. He contends that reporting abuse on a platform should not require membership on that platform.
key_points: Facebook's real-name policy combined with requiring signup to report impostors amounts to extortion of new users
Facebook responded to the impersonation report with bureaucratic runaround rather than action, despite clear evidence of impersonation
By claiming to enforce identity verification while allowing impostors, Facebook becomes liable for any slander committed under false identities
Abuse reporting systems should not require victims to contact abusers or become platform members to file reports
Safe Harbor protections may not apply when a platform exerts editorial control over identities but fails to enforce that control
money_quotes: If Facebook is claiming to require real names and identities on its platform, and requiring people to signup for Facebook to eliminate impostors, then they are extorting signups out of people.
Your only choice is to become a Facebook member and reserve your spot in their ad driven world even if you don't want to use their platform at all.
I should not have to navigate Facebook's problem reporting system to have this resolved.

Since Facebook refuses to take the account down and transfer control to me, and they claim to enforce identity giving visitors a false sense of security in trusting this impostor, then Facebook is actually the one causing the slander.
The entire point of having a system to report problems from abusive people is that I don't want to talk to the abusive person.
This extorting my eyeballs into viewing their shitty ads just to get an abusive asshole off my name is entirely wrong.
posts.key_insight: Platforms that claim to enforce real-name identity policies but require membership to report impostors are effectively extorting signups while enabling the very abuse they claim to prevent.

posts.summary: Shaw writes a satirical fake script for HBO's Silicon Valley as a critique of the show's portrayal of programmers. He exaggerates the show's recurring tropes — Richard's stuttering, physical incompetence, and submissiveness to business people — to absurd extremes to highlight what he sees as the show's reductive and demeaning depiction of technical people. The parody escalates from stuttering to self-mutilation, making the subtext of the show's dynamics explicit and grotesque. Shaw frames it as a job audition, but the piece is clearly a takedown of the show's reliance on emasculating programmer stereotypes for comedy.
key_points: Silicon Valley reduces programmers to pathetic, stuttering, physically incompetent stereotypes for laughs
The show reinforces a toxic alpha/beta dynamic where business people dominate and humiliate technical workers
The humor relies on making programmers increasingly emasculated and asexual as a running gag
Characters outside the white male nerd archetype are reduced to single-trait punchlines
Shaw uses escalating absurdity to make the show's implicit messaging uncomfortably explicit
money_quotes: It's really nailing the whole Male Nerd Beta dynamic and I feel that this is an area I can contribute my ample writing chops.
I'm usually spending my time writing books that turn normal people into stuttering, prat falling, pathetic nerds like on Silicon Valley.
I also can't really remember the names of all the characters but that's probably just my faulty memory circuits and has nothing to do with how memorable those characters are.
Now that's what I'm talking about! Richard you look even more like a programmer with your vagina mouth.
Everyone, bash your teeth so you have a vagina mouth and then cut your dicks off so that you look even more asexual and pathetic.
Good job asexuals! Way to be team players.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley's comedy depends on portraying programmers as pathetic, emasculated betas subordinate to alpha business types, and the show's writers keep finding new ways to degrade technical people for laughs.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that Riot Games violated California employment law by mining employees' in-game chat histories without their knowledge or consent, then using that data to identify employees for termination or reprimand. He details how the company analyzed past gameplay behavior, classified employees as toxic based on dubious sentiment analysis, and planned to extend this surveillance into their hiring process. Shaw enumerates eight specific legal and ethical violations, ranging from California Labor Code 980 to human research ethics requirements to discriminatory testing laws. He concludes that the practice deserves a class action lawsuit and criticizes the tech industry for celebrating corporate surveillance.
key_points: Riot mined employees' past in-game behavior without consent and used it to make firing decisions, likely violating California Labor Code 980
The study either violated human trials requirements by using real researchers or produced invalid results by using unqualified data scientists
The surveillance was applied asymmetrically — targeting employees who play the game for work while managers likely escaped scrutiny
Riot planned to extend this behavioral profiling into hiring decisions using a stoplight rating system in their applicant tracking system
Historical precedent with discriminatory employment tests like IQ tests suggests this kind of cultural behavioral test could be used to exclude people by gender or race
money_quotes: People were being identified based on either a flawed sentiment analysis of how snarky they were, or a manager's assessment of whether they were being snarky.

Either this study was done by amateurs who know nothing of sociology survey design and experimental design, or the study was done by trained Sociologists and those people have violated human trials requirements and should be held accountable.

An employer could have potentially recorded the audio of your home to figure out if they should fire you or not. Not even the government has that right so it's easy to say that no company should either.
Given enough time, Riot will find some dirt on any employee that they can use to fire anyone they like.
Programmers need to get their heads out of Ayn Rand's ass and stop worshipping corporations as some sort of infallible gods.
Corporations do not have your best interest at heart, and at best they're giving you a place to sit and do some job while they figure out how to fuck you over.
People will focus on the small percentage of toxic people and forget that Riot grabbed the chat logs of every person at the company to figure out who to fire without those employee's permission.
posts.key_insight: When a company surveils employees' personal behavior without consent and uses it for employment decisions, it's not innovative HR — it's a legal and ethical violation that the tech industry shouldn't celebrate.

posts.summary: Shaw shares his completion of a mastercopy of a John Singer Sargent painting, done as part of a class exercise. He notes an imperfection in the mouth of his copy and plans to attempt another Sargent piece, possibly in pastel. He explains the pedagogical purpose of mastercopying: learning how another artist approached their subject by studying brush strokes and color mixing embedded in the work.
key_points: Mastercopying is a method of learning another artist's technique by reproducing their work
Alla prima painting embeds the artist's history and technique in visible brush strokes and color choices
Working from an original painting is superior to working from a print for learning purposes
money_quotes: The purpose of a mastercopy is to learn how another artist would approach the subject.
Painting, especially alla prima, has the history and technique of the artist embedded in the painting.
You can figure out quite a bit of their style from the brush strokes and attempting to mix the same colors.
posts.key_insight: Mastercopying teaches an artist to reverse-engineer another painter's technique by physically recreating their brush strokes and color decisions.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw shares a brief progress update on his second cast drawing, depicting an old man he believes is a saint. He humorously dubs the piece 'Saint Anger' after the Metallica album he listened to while working. The drawing is done in pencil on Ampersand clay board. The post is a simple visual progress log with minimal commentary.
key_points: Shaw is practicing classical cast drawing techniques as part of his art education
He brings his characteristic humor to art practice, naming the piece after Metallica's album
The work demonstrates his commitment to traditional media (pencil on clay board)
money_quotes: I call him Saint Anger because I listened to mostly Metalica while I worked on it.
posts.key_insight: Shaw's art practice follows traditional atelier methods like cast drawing, approached with his signature irreverent humor.

posts.summary: Shaw documents a plein air painting session at the Willamette River during a brief trip to Portland, noting he's been too busy with writing and school to paint outdoors. He describes the challenges of painting in bright noon sunshine, which made everything too dark and the river too intensely blue. Back home, he experimented with scumbling and glazing techniques to correct the painting's issues. He treats the flawed painting as a learning opportunity, comparing the correction process to Photoshopping an image.
key_points: Plein air painting in bright noon light caused colors to appear too dark and overly saturated
Scumbling and glazing techniques can be used to fix paintings after the fact, adjusting value and intensity
Failed paintings serve as useful experiments for practicing correction techniques
Balancing creative pursuits with writing and education limits time for painting outdoors
money_quotes: I had painted it in the bright noon sunshine so everything was way too dark and the river was too intense blue.
I pushed the distant mountains back with more of a glaze of white and little bit of blue.
It's kind of like photoshopping it to see how you could fix it.
I put more intense green on the light side of the trees and finally glazed on some blue and transparent green and a little orange to make the water less intense.
I think I should have left out that weird spindly tree but I'll leave it and do something else with it in one more round.
posts.key_insight: Failed paintings become valuable learning experiments when approached as opportunities to practice correction techniques like scumbling and glazing.

posts.summary: Shaw shares the completion of a months-long cast drawing rendered in pencil on claybord, which he named 'Saint Anger' after the Metallica album. He explains that he names his cast drawings after metal albums because listening to metal keeps him sane during the meticulous rendering process. He outlines his next steps in classical art training, including Bargue plates and master copies, but declares he's done with pencil work.
key_points: Completed a detailed cast drawing that took months of meticulous pencil rendering on claybord
Uses metal album names for cast drawings as a personal coping mechanism for tedious work
Plans to progress through classical art training: Bargue plates, cast paintings, and master copies
Expresses frustration with pencil as a medium despite completing a disciplined piece
money_quotes: I name my cast drawings after metal albums because without metal I would go insane doing this meticulous rendering.
But fuck this pencil shit. Life's too short.
posts.key_insight: Shaw documents his serious commitment to classical art training while maintaining his characteristic irreverence about the process.

posts.summary: Shaw shares the completion of a six-month cast drawing of Saint Anger done on claybord using controlled pencil rendering. He discusses the properties of claybord as a drawing surface, noting its erasability and durability but warning it goes dark too easily. He outlines his next steps: moving to cast painting and Bargue plate copies rendered in paint rather than pencil, which he finds tedious. The post reflects a working artist methodically progressing through classical art training techniques.
key_points: Completed a 6-month cast drawing project requiring about 8 hours per week of controlled pencil rendering on claybord
Claybord offers indefinite erasability and durability but tends to go dark too easily, requiring extra work to maintain light values
Plans to shift from pencil rendering to paint rendering, viewing pencil work as tedious with diminishing learning value
Intends to return to Bargue plate copies to improve face drawing skills, but will render them in paint instead of pencil
money_quotes: This project took me 6 months to complete, working on it about 8 hours a week.
Claybord is great for pencil work. You can erase it indefinitely using various scratch board tools or normal erasers.
Rendering the lighter subtle value shifts requires erasing out the first rendering to pull it back to the light values.
Pencil rendering is tedious and annoying without much learning value.
I'm much more interested in learning to control values with paint so cast painting is my next step.
posts.key_insight: Classical art training is a methodical, long-term process where each completed project clarifies what medium and technique to pursue next.

posts.summary: Shaw shares his practice session with watercolor and ink, explaining the fundamental challenge of watercolor: it's transparent, so you paint light to dark using the paper as white. He walks through a simple three-step process for building watercolor paintings using value layers, starting with the lightest wash and progressively adding darker tones. He experiments with adding pen and ink to color watercolors but remains unconvinced about combining the two media.
key_points: Watercolor is the inverse of opaque paints because you build from light to dark, using paper as your white
Water control on the paper vs. brush determines edge quality — wet paper gives soft edges, dry paper gives hard edges
A three-step value layering process (white, light gray, mid-gray, then darks) provides a systematic approach to watercolor
Practicing with monochrome ink and water is the easiest way to learn watercolor fundamentals before adding color
money_quotes: Watercolor is the inverse of other paints because it's transparent so you build paintings from light to dark, using the paper as your white "pigment".
The easiest way to get used to this is to paint with just ink and water or any transparent black paint.
The advantage of watercolor is you can control the edges on these shapes if you dry the paper or not between each layer of paint.
If you have more water then the edges will be soft and out of focus. If you dry it then the edges will be hard and in focus.
Do these kind of monochrome paintings for a while and you'll get the hang of water as an edge control and using layers of watercolor to build the painting.
Those are birds or mosquitos or something annoying and tiny.
I really like ink for drawing and a brush pen to do monochrome paintings, but still not sure about it in watercolors.
posts.key_insight: Watercolor painting becomes approachable when you practice with monochrome ink first and think systematically about building value layers from light to dark.

posts.summary: Shaw documents a visit to the SF MOMA where he practiced photography composition using his Sony camera's High Contrast Black & White filter. He describes his workflow of using camera effects to find interesting shapes and compositions, then capturing RAW images for painting reference. The post is primarily a photo essay with brief captions describing his approach to each shot. He focuses on using the B&W filter to abstract subjects into pure shapes, whether people, sculptures, buildings, or neon signs.
key_points: Uses High Contrast B&W filter as a composition tool to identify interesting shapes before capturing RAW reference images for paintings
Modern art museums provide rich subjects for shape composition practice due to the mix of people, sculptures, and architectural elements
Deliberately tries to make photographed objects difficult to identify, abstracting them into pure shapes
Uses people as compositional elements to alter shapes within scenes
money_quotes: People standing around mixed with art sculptures and piles of garbage have great shapes.
I try to make it difficult to identify the object I'm photographing.
I used people to alter the shapes in the scene.
Modern art museums are great for this.
No idea if I'm violating copyright but whatever.
posts.key_insight: Black and white high-contrast photography can serve as a practical composition tool for painters, stripping scenes down to their essential shapes before capturing detailed color reference.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that the programming community's ability to influence careers and products through platforms like Reddit and Hacker News has effectively ended. He uses his own experience as evidence: after Reddit removed his Python book from recommended reading lists and online mobs attacked his criticism of Python 3, his traffic and sales remained unchanged. He contends that the previous generation of programmers consistently backed terrible ideas based on tribal loyalty rather than merit, and that a newer generation of programmers is rejecting this tribal, hive-mind culture. He concludes by encouraging creators to ignore the online mob and keep making things.
key_points: Reddit's removal of Shaw's book from recommended Python resources had zero measurable impact on his sales or traffic
Python 3 has persistent usability problems for beginners—particularly around strings and error messages—that the community refuses to address, preferring to pressure authors instead
The programmer hive mind consistently backs bad technology choices based on tribal identity rather than technical merit
The newer generation of programmers is more well-rounded and less susceptible to tribal groupthink
Online outrage from platforms like HN and Reddit has far less real-world influence than people fear
money_quotes: The reddit community responsible for teaching beginners to code censored my book as a power play to get me to force Python 3 on unsuspecting beginners.
I decided to just write what I felt and fuck whatever programmers think.
Who gives a fuck about what a bunch of angry lonely coders think about my thoughts?
I've heard of VCs actually threatening to strip away funding over bad HN reactions like HN is on the same level as the food critic of the NYT.
They ran to Node.js because of 'events are better than threads' and had no idea Hoare or coroutines existed.
For all their claims of superiority for having once bought a copy of The Art of Computer Programming the previous generation of programmers are sadly uninformed about basic shit.
I believe that the influence of the previous generation of programmers is largely gone.
Keep making cool stuff and speaking your mind counter to the hive.
posts.key_insight: The tech community's online outrage machine has far less real-world power than people believe, and creators should stop fearing it and build what they want.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw announces his move from San Francisco to Miami, reflecting on his seven years in the Bay Area with mixed feelings. He credits San Francisco for launching his book career, learning to paint, and memorable moments at Union Square and Fort Mason, but criticizes the city's toxic culture war between tech bros and anti-tech libertines. He chose Miami for its lack of state income tax (important for his plan to study art overseas), its art scene, and its generally happier vibe. The post is structured as a farewell love letter to San Francisco that doubles as an indictment of the city's personality.
key_points: San Francisco's culture is ruined by the constant war between brogrammers riding tech wealth and anti-tech libertines, making it an ugly place to live and work
Shaw's time in SF was personally transformative — he built a writing career, learned to paint, and healed his guitar-playing hands at Union Square
California's aggressive overseas tax collection policies were a practical factor in leaving the state
Shaw personifies cities as lovers and personalities: NYC as a bipolar court jester, SF as a depressed brilliant failed artist, Miami as someone too fabulous to care
Miami was chosen for art development, lower cost, better weather, and proximity to overseas relocation plans
money_quotes: As the money in tech prolapsed into the local economy the brogrammers in the industry confused their acquisition of a loan with being real super dudes.
This constant fight between the wannabe libertines just trying to get whipped at The Citadel, and the brogrammers living off Zuck's scraps, ends up ruining what could be a fantastic place to live.
San Francisco will always be that lover who made me feel awesome and successful while cheating on me and calling me a 'fag nerd'.
