The personality of New York was a bipolar court jester. There were days you could tell NYC was pissed off at you, other days where Gotham loved you like a mother, and most of the time it was fucking hilarious.
San Francisco's personality is definitely that of a depressed but brilliant failed artist.
When you live in a city you can sort of feel a personality permeating everything.
I don't know what Miami is like yet, but I'm getting the sense that Miami actually doesn't give a fuck what I think about her. Miami is looking too fabulous to care.
posts.key_insight: Cities shape the people who live in them, and sometimes the healthiest thing is to leave before you fully absorb a city's dysfunction.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that 'magic' — the mystical aura surrounding any skill or technology — is fundamentally a con that obscures reality and enslaves people. He recounts telling a musician friend that learning theory wouldn't kill her artistry but reveal something more beautiful. He extends this to technology, arguing that much of the magic around programming languages and companies hides ugly designs and poor implementations. Shaw frames his life's mission as stripping away magic through education, citing his 'Learn Code the Hard Way' books as proof that programming isn't reserved for special geniuses. He concludes that most practitioners don't intentionally gatekeep — they simply cargo-cult rituals they mistake for essential practice.
key_points: Magic is a con that makes people love the illusion rather than the real thing, which is always simpler and more beautiful
Much of the magic in technology hides terrible design, poor implementation, and random warts rather than genuine beauty
Education is the practice of breaking magic to expose reality, opening skills to a wider range of people
Most practitioners aren't intentionally gatekeeping — they cargo-cult rituals they believe are secret sauce but are really extraneous
Magic enslaves people by making them dependent on wizards to define their reality
money_quotes: Magic just hides something's true beauty. It's a con. A trick that makes you love the magic rather than the real thing.
Once you actually learn how it really works, sure, the magic goes away, but then you get to fall in love with the beauty of the real thing.
It seems everyone in technology aspires to nothing more than creating enough of a code mannequin to hold up an invisible emperor's gown.
I believe magic enslaves people to others.
I proved with my books that there really is no magic to learning to code. The people who could do it weren't special geniuses.
Education then becomes the practice of breaking magic to expose reality.
Most practitioners simply cargo cult a set of random practices they're sure are the secret sauce.
posts.key_insight: The mystique surrounding any skill or technology serves to enslave people to gatekeepers, and education's true purpose is to strip away that mystique so anyone can access the real, simpler thing underneath.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that copying and repetition (rote learning) are the foundation of all education, yet computer science uniquely dismisses these methods in favor of conceptual learning. He draws parallels to music, painting, writing, and martial arts, where copying masters and repetitive practice are universally accepted as essential. He contends that conceptual-first CS education acts as a weed-out system rather than genuinely teaching beginners. Shaw proposes adopting music-style pedagogy for beginners (scales, copying, modification) and painting-style pedagogy for intermediates (master copies and studies), arguing this would make programming accessible to more people.
key_points: Conceptual learning models don't actually teach — they identify students who already know the material and leave the rest behind
Every creative discipline (music, painting, writing, martial arts) relies on copying and repetition as foundational pedagogy, disproving the claim that rote learning kills creativity
Experienced programmers forget they learned through rote copying and therefore dismiss it as a teaching method
CS education should adopt a music model for beginners (copying and drilling) and a painting model for intermediates (master copies and personal studies)
The dismissal of rote learning in favor of conceptual purity has a subtly racist dimension when applied to characterize entire cultures
money_quotes: The reality is conceptual models of education simply find students lucky enough to naturally know the topic, and then leave the rest to fail and flounder.
The idea that I'm going to learn the major scale on a guitar by just learning the concept of a major scale is laughable.
If doing rote copying turned painters or musicians into unoriginal robots then all of them would be that way.
The dividing line between amateur and professional is how much they practice, and practice is repetition.
Copying is how a vast majority of programmers learned to code, but many CS educators deny this fact.
They are so far removed from their beginner experience that they forget that they actually learned to code via rote learning.
The experienced programmer has completely forgotten the nights they spent repeatedly copying other people's code and writing and rewriting buggy code to make it work.
Denying the role of these two practices in education denies what is a foundational aspect of human learning.
posts.key_insight: Computer science education's rejection of copying and repetition in favor of conceptual learning is an anomaly among creative disciplines, and adopting the proven rote-then-create pedagogy of music and painting would make programming far more accessible to beginners.

posts.summary: Shaw recounts his experience at an art school where a teacher systematically undermined him from the moment he arrived. The teacher mocked his brushes, failed to warn him about technique requirements until it was too late, and ultimately spent an entire lunch trying to convince Shaw to quit studying art—all triggered by a student's outrage over his joke Bob Ross paintings. Shaw identifies the school's culture as cult-like, where deviation from orthodoxy provokes hostile gatekeeping. He credits his own stubbornness for surviving the encounter but notes most students would have been permanently derailed.
key_points: Art education institutions can develop cult-like cultures where any deviation from approved methods is treated as threatening
The teacher employed a pattern of passive-aggressive undermining rather than direct instruction or honest feedback
A fellow student's extreme reaction to joke paintings reveals how deeply institutional orthodoxy can be internalized
Gatekeeping in creative education can permanently destroy students' aspirations under the guise of guidance
Surviving toxic mentorship requires personal resilience that shouldn't be a prerequisite for learning
money_quotes: I have literally met the man for an hour and he's already insulting my gear.
I look around the room. I'm the only one with a dark grisaille. Why didn't he tell me that before the paint dried?
She immediately points to the middle of the painting and say, "What?! You can't do that!"
He spends the entire lunch trying to convince me to not attend this school or study their methods because of my Bob Ross paintings.
You're right. This place is a damn cult. There's no way I'd study here if people react this way to a joke Bob Ross painting.
It took him an hour to gradually crush my aspirations to be an artist, and it almost worked.
Thankfully, I have a high dose of "Fuck You" in my blood to counteract people like that.
That kind of interaction would have derailed many students permanently.
posts.key_insight: Art education institutions can become gatekeeping cults where teachers crush students who deviate from orthodoxy, and surviving such environments shouldn't require extraordinary personal resilience.

posts.summary: Shaw recounts three experiences with art teachers who prioritize performative emotional attachment and mystical discovery over practical skill development. In the first vignette, he's attacked for honestly critiquing a student's video installation piece. In the second, a teacher scolds him for painting noses as practice rather than fabricating a deep personal connection to his subject. In the third, a teacher uses distracting colored lights and music while simultaneously demanding accuracy, then admits she never uses these techniques herself. Together, these stories illustrate Shaw's frustration with art education that values pretentious narrative over craft and honest engagement.
key_points: Art school culture demands performative emotional attachment to work, punishing honest or practical motivations
Students learn to game the system by fabricating deep personal narratives about mundane subjects
Teachers impose contradictory demands—be loose and expressive but also accurate—without acknowledging the contradiction
When pressed, the teacher admits she doesn't use her own methods, exposing the gap between what's taught and what's practiced
money_quotes: Art is always a piece. Artists are never "popular", they're always "important".
I just stand there and take it, since I'm outnumbered 1 cynic to 12 true believers.
The experienced students know the game. They effuse wildly about their personal connection to the subject.
I say, "I wanted to practice noses." She scowls at me and says, "It seems like you aren't personally attached to your subject."
I confusedly pause then ask, "I'm not personally attached to my face?"
"Because you have to discover that for yourself." Well then why am I paying you money?
I ask if she does this with her paintings and she says, "Oh no, not at all."
posts.key_insight: Art education often rewards performative emotional narratives and mystical discovery rituals while punishing students who approach their craft with honesty and practical intent.

posts.summary: Shaw documents his experiment combining memory drawing techniques with iterative refinement processes from traditional art training. He traces the history of memory drawing back to Père Lecoq's 1848 French Academy experiments, then describes his own methodology: staring at a scene, covering it, drawing from memory, then checking accuracy and repeating with finer detail. He tests this approach across multiple media (charcoal, pastel, oil paint) and drawing styles (relative measurement, block-in, painterly), finding that pastels and the block-in/painterly combination work best. The results surprised him with their accuracy even on early rounds, and the process felt meditative compared to conventional constant-reference drawing.
key_points: There are four main approaches to representational drawing: sight-sized, relative measurement, block-in, and painterly, each with distinct tradeoffs in accuracy, speed, and equipment needs
Memory drawing, pioneered by Père Lecoq in 1848, can produce results comparable to conventional methods when combined with iterative refinement rather than expecting perfect recall
Shaw's key innovation is applying 'memory chunking' — memorizing large shapes first, then progressively smaller details across multiple rounds — rather than trying to memorize an entire scene at once
The medium matters: pastels and pre-mixed oil paints work better than charcoal because they allow more direct value matching to what's held in memory
Drawing accuracy in general is more a product of time spent refining than any immediate magical precision — 99.9% of accurate artists start with messy guesses
money_quotes: It seems impossible, but you can actually stare at a scene or a photo, and after about 2-3 minutes of staring at it, do a fairly close approximation of it.
The process of drawing is actually one of refinement from a gross mistake. You start off with a guess that's unrefined and messy or bulky.
99.9% of all artists who paint or draw very accurately either copy photos, start of with fairly messy guesses that become accurate, or don't actually draw as accurately as you think they do.
I had to stop and slow down and stare at this photo or object until it melted away and turned into a group of shapes.
It was nearly the same sensation as meditation, and then when I went to paint it was like releasing a breath. As if what I memorized had to come out now.
When I paint or draw other ways it's more like I'm pulling and working a large rope to pull the drawing onto the canvas.
Accuracy is more a product of time spent refining than any sort of immediate magical accuracy.
posts.key_insight: Drawing accuracy comes not from magical precision but from iterative refinement, and memory drawing combined with chunked memorization and gradual correction can match conventional methods while feeling more meditative and direct.

posts.summary: Shaw reflects on a failed flower painting to argue that talent is irrelevant to enjoying creative pursuits. He describes his lack of natural ability at painting, explaining that his skills come entirely from reading books and deliberate practice rather than innate gift. He credits painting, particularly self-portraits and landscapes, with saving his life during a dark period. The core message is that having zero talent is actually liberating because it frees you to focus on the process rather than the result.
key_points: Skills that appear to be talent are often just reading books and doing what they say — including seemingly impressive feats like painting from memory
Having zero talent is liberating because it removes pressure about outcomes and lets you focus on the process
Painting self-portraits and landscapes served as therapeutic tools that helped Shaw through depression
The belief that you need talent to enjoy something prevents people from ever starting
money_quotes: I wouldn't say I have any particular talent at painting. I'm definitely not a genius and I put in a lot of work to be even moderately alright at it.
That's not talent. That's just being able to read some books and do what they say.
Everyone could learn how to do it if they put in the time and believed they could.
I could give a fuck if I have any talent or not. I'm enjoying myself and I get to give them to friends.
Self-portraits and landscape painting quite literally saved my life at a time when everything was sad and dark.
Doing paintings of my face helped me reconnect with who I am. Painting outside got me out of bed.
Having zero talent is liberating. You can stop worrying about the end result and just enjoy the process and experience.
It really doesn't matter if what you create is any good. All that matters is if you learn something for the next time.
posts.key_insight: Talent is neither required nor desirable for enjoying a creative pursuit — liberation comes from accepting you have none and focusing entirely on the process and what you learn.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw details his conflict with the Python Software Foundation after publicly criticizing Python 3, claiming PSF members actively tried to censor his book and damage his livelihood. He outlines anticipated PSF defenses and systematically rebuts each one, arguing that the PSF's Code of Conduct is meaningless if never enforced. Shaw contends that his treatment demonstrates a broader pattern where dissenting opinions in the Python community are met with abuse rather than constructive engagement. He warns other Python developers that if the PSF is willing to target someone with his stature, smaller contributors are even more vulnerable.
key_points: PSF members allegedly used their positions to discourage people from reading Shaw's book and contacted other authors to have references to it removed
Shaw argues the PSF's Code of Conduct is performative since the organization never enforces it against members who harass dissenters
He presents a logical trap: if Python 3 is similar to Python 2, he's qualified to teach it; if it's too hard for him, that proves his criticism
Shaw claims prominent Python programmers privately agree with his criticisms but won't speak publicly for fear of similar retaliation
He frames the conflict as a trust issue for anyone considering investing in Python as a language or community
money_quotes: Imagine thousands of Python programmers deciding that you don't deserve to work and make a living because you don't like Python 3.
If it is that easy to become a PSF member, then you should not allow people to tell anyone they are a PSF member until they've earned some sort of rank.
Given that, it must be that Python 3 is too difficult to use if I'm not qualified to teach it.
Without a solid statement against such behavior by PSF members the PSF board is allowing the behavior that is convenient to them.
I donated a book for free that was read by 12.5 million people on my single small website alone.
Several prominent Python programmers were willing to tell me privately they agree with me, but won't publicly because of this kind of abuse.
All because I don't like Python 3's strings.
If they're willing to do this to me then who knows what they'll do to someone like you.
posts.key_insight: Organizations that adopt codes of conduct but never enforce them against members who harass dissenters create environments where honest criticism becomes professionally dangerous.

posts.summary: Shaw addresses the death threats and suicide encouragement he receives from programming communities angered by his opinions on languages like Python 3. He traces the psychology of online abuse from IRC hostility toward beginners to escalating bullying that can drive people to suicide, drawing parallels between tech community harassment and broader patterns of sociopathic abuse. He argues that online communities collectively designate acceptable targets, giving sociopaths permission to escalate harassment. Shaw refuses to be intimidated, asserting his right to self-defense and declaring that his life is thriving despite the threats.
key_points: Programming communities collectively designate 'acceptable targets' for abuse, from beginners asking questions in IRC to public figures who disagree with language orthodoxy
Online bullying follows a predictable escalation pattern from name-calling to humiliation to pushing vulnerable people toward suicide, and perpetrators never show genuine remorse
The trivial pretexts for abuse—disagreeing about Python 3, criticizing Ruby culture—reveal that the reason never actually matters; any excuse satisfies the abuser's need
Violent and sociopathic people do not respond to kind words or reason, and Shaw argues self-defense against threats is morally justified
Shaw refuses to be intimidated, framing resilience and a good life as the ultimate rebuke to anonymous harassers
money_quotes: People are so attached to their programming languages that they even hate newbies who just don't ask the right questions in IRC.
If the vast majority of your authority in life comes from ASCII text caps lock screaming at people just trying to learn something then you have got to be one of the biggest losers out there.
Once the larger group decides that this person is a valid target the sociopaths take their cue and feel justified in their abuse.
It was not a game. It was not an accident. It was a calculated act following a calculated chain of abuse designed to destroy another human being for no real reason.
All the victim actually did was exist and happen to give their murderer or abuser an excuse to exercise his or her desire to satisfy their narcissistic need to hurt someone that day.

I know this person is a pathetic fucking loser. I know they wrote that hoping I would die, hoping to kill me, but needed an excuse, and my disagreement with this totally inert concept called Python/Ruby/Haskell is all they needed.
Their community has crafted the right amount of propaganda and cohesion to allow any decision about my 'asshole' status be the abuser's cue that I'm fair game.
posts.key_insight: Programming communities that collectively designate acceptable targets for hatred—whether beginners or dissenters—are enabling the same escalation patterns that drive real-world abuse and tragedy.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw explores the surprisingly difficult challenge of photographing paintings accurately. He explains that cameras' automatic corrections, light color temperature, specular highlights, and exposure issues all conspire to produce inaccurate reproductions. After extensive experimentation with various techniques, he settles on a process using a tripod, zoom lens, Expodisc for white balance calibration, and multiple exposures—though he admits he usually skips most of it out of laziness.
key_points: Camera auto white balance and social media compression actively work against accurate art reproduction, making phone snapshots unreliable for documenting paintings
Human color perception is subjective enough that no photograph of a painting will ever satisfy every viewer—you can only get close to accuracy, never achieve it
Most photography advice fails for art because photographers optimize for pleasing images while art documentation requires forensic accuracy
The Expodisc ($44) outperforms expensive color checker grids ($250+) for white balance calibration when photographing paintings
Instagram and Twitter actively degrade art photos through compression, color manipulation, and cropping that ignores centuries of painting aspect ratios
money_quotes: When photographing artwork (or any forensic photography), your ideas of artistic integrity do not belong. You're trying to record as exactly as possible what is photographed.
Rather than try to take a photo correctly the first time, a photographer will simply take a whole bunch of photos and then fix them all up in Photoshop from memory.
The end result is you can never really get a photo of a painting perfectly accurate so that everyone looking at it in every kind of light will say it's 'correct'. You just have to get close.
It's almost as if they simply went and tested two images from a 35mm camera and then forgot about 2000 years of painting history or all medium format cameras and beyond.
How else am I going to get 20 likes on Instagram?!
If you have a low quality image to start with that has a lot of noise, then most of the things you do to try and fix it simply make it worse.
posts.key_insight: Photographing paintings is a forensic documentation problem, not an artistic one, and most photography tools and platforms are designed to make images look pleasing rather than accurate.

posts.summary: Shaw defends 'BrandonM,' a Hacker News commenter whose 2007 skeptical comment about Dropbox was publicly mocked by Drew Houston and Sam Altman over a decade later. Shaw argues this represents a power imbalance where billionaires punch down at ordinary people who can't even delete their old comments. He critiques Y Combinator's refusal to allow comment deletion as abusive and contradictory to their professed progressive values. Shaw then methodically analyzes BrandonM's original comment, arguing it was actually reasonable given the 2007 technology landscape. The core argument is that Silicon Valley elites demand openness to criticism but weaponize their power against anyone who once doubted them.
key_points: Drew Houston and Sam Altman used their massive platforms to publicly humiliate an ordinary person over an 11-year-old comment, representing a grotesque power imbalance
Hacker News's refusal to let users delete their own comments is abusive and potentially violates EU right-to-be-forgotten laws
Y Combinator's claimed progressive values contradict their actual behavior regarding diversity, harassment, and punching down at powerless critics
BrandonM's 2007 Dropbox criticism was actually technically valid for the time—and Dropbox still doesn't turn a profit, proving his point about income generation
Silicon Valley success is less about technical merit and more about social connections through elite schools and fraternity networks
money_quotes: A man who just became a billionaire took time out of his day to hunt down a comment by a regular nobody 11 years later and throw his success back in that guy's face.
We can criticize you, but you better not criticize us.
They're big fans of the whole 'waiting for your body on the river Ganges' thing, and if you ever once doubted them they'll hunt you down after their success just to shit on you.
They totally made it on the glory of their shitty code and that's why your technical comments deserve retribution.
Progressive or liberal people do not take advantage of their wealth and power to humiliate someone less powerful than they are over something this petty.
The most important sign that YC is an abusive company is the fact that they refuse to allow people to delete comments even a decade later.
They don't really believe that people make mistakes—oops sorry, founders make mistakes all the time and get away with it. They just don't believe me and you are allowed to make mistakes.
He was right and it could have gone totally differently with just one wrong move.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley billionaires weaponize old criticisms against powerless individuals while hiding behind progressive values they don't actually practice.

posts.summary: Zed Shaw documents his migration away from WordPress to a custom blog built with Svelte and Sapper. After using WordPress since 2014 while he studied painting, he grew frustrated with its poor media handling and confusing UI. He credits Svelte's reactive model and the Zettlr writing tool as the catalysts for rebuilding his web presence. The post reflects on how maintaining custom blog software keeps programming skills sharp, something he regrets neglecting during his painting years.
key_points: WordPress is adequate for text but terrible for media, organization, and anything beyond basic blogging
Maintaining custom blog software is a valuable way to stay sharp as a programmer and keep up with technology curves
Svelte's 'assignment is reactive' model represents a significant leap in UI development simplicity
Good writing tools matter: Zettlr's note organization and focus mode enable a better writing workflow than browser-based editors
Six years studying painting improved his visual and UI design abilities
money_quotes: Thinking back this was a terrible idea because my programming skills didn't have anywhere to exercise, and a blog is an excellent way to stay sharp.
You get sucked into doing something that's barely capable and there's not much of a reason to change even if it could be better.
I really want everyone to adopt the 'assignment is reactive' model in the near future and feel that Rich is a genius.
It's so simple I'm constantly asking, 'Why didn't I think of that.'
Just the fact that the backspace key can cause all of my work to disappear gives me hives.
I suspect also my 6+ years studying painting have made my visual abilities stronger so I'm able to handle the UI much better than before.
posts.key_insight: Outsourcing your tools to platforms like WordPress trades short-term convenience for long-term skill atrophy and creative limitation.

posts.summary: Shaw explores painting as a practice of disabling the brain's automatic pattern recognition to see raw visual reality. He examines three properties of paintings—energy, space, and magic—arguing that energy comes from loose, intuitive brushwork, space determines how much room a painting leaves for viewer interpretation, and magic is a false mysticism people project onto a learnable skill. He draws an analogy between tightly defined research papers and open-ended poetry to explain how paintings can either dictate meaning or invite the viewer in. He concludes that the real 'magic' of painting is the deliberate exploitation of visual perception to create experiences, not some spiritual gift.
key_points: Learning to paint requires temporarily disabling the brain's automatic labeling system that converts visual input into words and categories
Energy in paintings comes from looseness and intuition—less defined work paradoxically feels more alive than precise rendering
Paintings exist on a spectrum from 'research paper' (leaving no room for interpretation) to 'poem' (creating space for the viewer's feelings), and skilled artists should be able to work in both modes
The mystification of art as spiritual magic serves as a barrier to entry, when painting is actually a learnable skill that most people can acquire with effort
The real craft of painting is exploiting human visual deficiencies and perceptual shortcuts to create controlled illusions
money_quotes: I have to focus to disable the part of my brain that is trying to nonchalantly glance at the photons entering my optic nerve and slap the word 'flower' on it then move on.
You can always scrape that shit off and try again tomorrow. The end result mostly doesn't matter.
Paintings full of energy have inaccuracies in their attempt to depict this charge.
A poem attempts to leave no concept defined. A poem is the undefined.
I have no choice but to leave a space for humans in my art because they will bull their way into my finely crafted colorful tea shop whether I like it or not.
People will see magic in anything they don't understand, so many painters simply go with it.

The truth is, painting is not that complex. It's difficult mostly because people's brains don't actually work well for the task, so it requires training to repurpose their mental machinery into seeing in a new way.
I can trick their brain into seeing what I saw, or seeing what I want them to see, with a pigmented sleight of hand and misdirection of light bouncing off color sheets of dried nuts.
posts.key_insight: Painting is not mystical talent but a learnable skill of temporarily overriding the brain's automatic perception, and its real power lies in deliberately exploiting visual cognition to create controlled experiences for viewers.

posts.summary: Shaw recounts a plein air painting expedition through Tbilisi, Georgia during a round-the-world trip, walking miles on a repeatedly injured left ankle while carrying 25 pounds of painting and camera gear. He describes his obsessive dedication to outdoor painting as a personal compulsion, detailing his equipment setup, the Georgian food and culture he fell in love with, and his discovery of the perfect painting spot on a pedestrian walkway near the Bridge of Peace. The piece builds from travelogue into a meditation on the emotional process of translating a scene into paint, using palette knives to capture the swirling contrast of sadness at leaving Tbilisi mixed with excitement about returning home. Written entirely from memory, it captures the intersection of physical suffering, artistic obsession, and the bittersweet end of a journey before 2020 changed everything.
key_points: Shaw carried 25 pounds of painting and camera gear across multiple countries on a repeatedly injured ankle, driven by an irrepressible need to paint outdoors
The logistics of plein air painting gear — weight optimization, packing strategy, camera setup for filming sessions — become their own form of creative problem-solving
Georgian food and culture made Tbilisi his favorite city, with its unique blend of Indian, Turkish, and French culinary influences
The actual painting process involves deep emotional study of a scene, translating feelings into technical decisions about brushwork versus palette knife
The piece captures a specific moment of bittersweet transition — the end of a transformative trip just before 2020 upended everything
money_quotes: I desperately needed to keep going outside and painting. It was my white whale. My dragon. I had to chase the elusive experience of painting in the perfect spot at the perfect time.
When I'm painting I just don't care about what's around me, and I was loving the scene, but that was stupid.
Fuck that ankle. I gotta paint. I can just go back to Thailand and get ankle surgery next year.
Packing gear for plein air painting is a form of artistic expression almost as great as the actual painting.
It's nuts and I love it. I could just go out and paint but fuck that, this is a sport for me. A challenge. How can I make this more fun and interesting?
The knife is energy. It's accidents and randomness from the lack of control. A knife leaves sharp paint, like slivers of pigmented glass sprinkled all over the canvas.
A calm quiet cold morning, a bar playing music, the sadness of leaving a place I love, but a hopeful future before everything changed in 2020.
posts.key_insight: Artistic obsession overrides physical pain and practical sense, because the compulsion to capture a perfect moment in paint is its own reward that no injury can diminish.

posts.summary: Shaw draws a parallel between the fear-driven perfectionism he observed in classical realist painters and the shame-based culture of the programming industry. He argues that for 30 years, corporations and open source leaders have weaponized shame to control programmers, creating an internalized authoritarianism that squeezes creative space out of code. This realization crystallizes when he encounters Nim's style insensitivity feature, which allows flexible naming conventions, and has a visceral negative reaction he can't rationally justify. He recognizes his reaction as the same authoritarian impulse he criticizes, and resolves to try Nim as an act of defiance against rigid programming culture. The post connects his artistic journey toward leaving space in paintings with a desire for the same creative freedom in programming.
key_points: The programming industry has spent 30 years using shame to control developers, creating generations of programmers who live in constant fear of making mistakes
Programmers accept criticism and shaming from corporations like Google but reject the same from peers, revealing an internalized authoritarian hierarchy
Open source codes of conduct often replicate the authoritarianism they claim to oppose, with secret enforcers, no elections, and unequal enforcement
Shaw's visceral rejection of Nim's style insensitivity feature revealed his own internalized authoritarianism about how code 'must' be written
The concept of 'space' from art — leaving room for interpretation rather than enforcing exactness — is what programming culture desperately lacks
money_quotes: For the last 30 years nearly every major movement in software has been an attempt to shame programmers into feeling inadequate for a situation that is entire out of their control.
We'll all accept shaming from the corporations in power, but then reject it when it's from a peer? It should be the inverse.
The authoritarianism in programming is so pervasive and internalized that I'm guilty of this hypocrisy myself.
Through art I've begun to realize how my blueprints of computational expression were founded on a slab of totalitarian thought.
I fully internalized the idea that space in a software system leads to disaster.
By hating on this feature I'm removing space that Nim is trying to create.
It's so difficult to scrub out things that you believe kept you safe all these years.
I'd love to be able to just write code the way I just paint. Just a little.
posts.key_insight: The programming industry's culture of shame and rigid correctness is an internalized authoritarianism that kills creative expression, and recognizing it in yourself is the first step toward writing code with the same freedom you'd bring to art.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that Google's free programming courses marketed as bachelor's degree equivalents are performative rather than genuinely helpful to the poor, noting that Google ignored community colleges and didn't offer free MBA programs that would give actual power to disadvantaged people. He then turns to what he calls 'Empathy Gatekeepers'—people who demand programmers take extensive ethics and soft skills courses while ignoring the complete absence of such requirements in Google's program. Shaw contends that demands for programmer ethics training are really about control and political signaling, not about producing better people, pointing out that history's worst actors were trained in soft skills, not programming. He concludes that if ethics advocates were serious, they'd hold corporations and managers accountable first rather than targeting powerless working programmers.
key_points: Google's free programming courses bypass community colleges and avoid replacing MBA programs, revealing the initiative is about optics rather than genuinely empowering the poor
The same people who celebrate Google's abbreviated degree also demand extensive ethics coursework for programmers, exposing a contradiction in their gatekeeping logic
Ethics and empathy education does not reliably produce ethical people—history's worst atrocities were committed by people skilled in soft skills like oratory, writing, and politics
Demands for programmer ethics training are really about power and political conformity, not about improving technology's impact on society
Programmers are targeted because they're perceived as weak and easily manipulated, while corporations and managers who hold actual power face no such ethical demands
money_quotes: Google doesn't actually want to help the poor. Google wants to just look like they're helping the poor with the least probability that anyone who's actually poor would gain any amount of power.
Where's the course that replaces an MBA? If there's one degree that is totally worthless it's the Masters of Business Administration.

The education of ethics and empathy does not remove a sociopath's desire for power and abuse, and it doesn't make anyone a better person. It mostly only makes people better at appearing to be a better person.
Quite often the people with the best soft skills end up being the most evil, fucked up, narcissistic pieces of shit you've ever known.
Ethics and Empathy don't give a fuck about your politics.

It's entirely unethical and lacking of empathy to go to a group of people who are at a power disadvantage in their jobs and demand that they take extra classes to learn to be even better at doing what they're told.

The Empathy Gatekeepers sit silent when corporations and managers are unethical, because if the Empathy Gatekeepers said anything bad then none of the FAANGs would hire them to be a totally useless Developer Evangelists.
95% of programmers aren't in charge of anything. Programmers have largely been considered replaceable worker drone cogs throughout the history of Programming as a profession.
posts.key_insight: Demands for programmer ethics education are a form of power politics that targets workers with no institutional power while giving a pass to the corporations and managers who actually make unethical decisions.

posts.summary: Shaw argues that authoritarianism is deeply embedded in software development communities, particularly open source projects. He defines authoritarianism as deference to authority given or taken without enthusiastic informed consent, and separates analysis of authoritarian leaders from authoritarian communities. Using the Python community as his primary case study, he contrasts the treatment of PSF leader Steve Holden—who allegedly asked women to touch a sexually suggestive prop at PyCon 2012 and faced no consequences—with the immediate ejection and job losses of two attendees who made a private dongle joke at PyCon 2013. Shaw contends this disparity in punishment demonstrates clear deference to authority, proving the Python community and its leadership operate as an authoritarian system.
key_points: Authoritarianism in open source is defined by deference to authority without enthusiastic informed consent, not by violence or fascism
Authoritarian communities and authoritarian leaders must be analyzed separately, as either can exist without the other
The Python community's unequal enforcement of its Code of Conduct—protecting leaders while harshly punishing ordinary members—demonstrates textbook deference to authority
Codes of conduct in open source can function as tools of authoritarian control when written to protect those in power while enabling totalitarian punishment of dissenters
The concept of 'enthusiastic informed consent' should be the standard for evaluating whether participation in open source communities is truly voluntary
money_quotes: It's my belief that authoritarianism is so ingrained in the fabric of software development that it has been internalized to such a degree that even the most anti-authoritarian people cannot avoid being authoritarian.
I believe there is no such thing as a Benevolent Dictator for Life, and that by learning why that's true you can better decide for yourself how you want to spend your free time.
Any deference to authority given or taken without the enthusiastic informed consent of everyone involved.
It's authoritarian duck-typing, where we say, if the community quacks like an authoritarian duck, then the organization is authoritarian.
Not being as bad as Hitler is easily the lowest bar possible for ethical behavior of a leader.
When the community allows the leaders to violate rules, but attacks and destroys random tangential people for lesser violations, you have clear deference to authority and that's authoritarianism.
Prominent Social Justice Warriors, Feminists, and Anti-authoritarians not only didn't go after Steve for his vile behavior, but they defended him at every turn.
The issue here is not so much the severity of both offenses, but more that the punishment was totalitarian and unequally applied based on a deference to Steve's authority.
posts.key_insight: Open source communities that claim to be meritocratic and egalitarian often function as authoritarian systems where rules are enforced harshly on ordinary members but waived for those in positions of power, and the community's willingness to accept this double standard is itself the core mechanism of authoritarianism.

posts.summary: Shaw tells the story of receiving a custom titanium palette knife he ordered four years ago and forgot about, then immediately damaging it through an unnecessary repair attempt. He applied permanent Locktite to a perfectly fine screw, couldn't remove it, and scored the polished titanium pommel with pliers. He then redeemed himself by progressively sanding and polishing the damage away, making a creative connection between titanium polishing and a guitar fret polishing cloth he had lying around. The story serves as a self-portrait of his personality: impulsive destruction followed by resourceful repair.
key_points: Shaw uses palette knives rather than brushes for oil painting, preferring their aggressive, energetic mark-making over the softer results of brushwork
His custom knife features a color-matching hole in the blade and a lion chop signature stamp, both reflecting his systematic yet personal approach to art
ADHD drives both his impulsive decision to fix something that wasn't broken and his ability to forget a major purchase for four years
The rescue came from an unlikely cross-domain connection: a dried-out guitar fret polishing cloth solved a metalworking problem
Shaw frames his identity as the cycle of idiotic destruction followed by creative problem-solving
money_quotes: The knife is justice. Retribution. Correction.
Then the knife looks up at me and says, 'Hey buddy. Wanna cause some trouble?'

I have ADHD and it's an expensive neurological condition that combines lowered inhibition that makes me buy an expensive custom palette with an inability to remember that I paid a ton of money for an expensive custom palette knife.
I am a fucking dumbass. I knew this would happen? What was I thinking?

I'm an idiot for thinking any of the above things would work, but I'm a genius for making the obscure connection between polishing this palette knife, and a polishing cloth that is used on guitar frets.
I love the knife even more because now it has a story. And it doesn't look like crap because I was an idiot.
This is me. The idiot genius.
posts.key_insight: Shaw's identity is the cycle of impulsive destruction and resourceful repair — the same recklessness that creates problems also enables the cross-domain thinking that solves them.

posts.summary: Shaw recounts how survival crafting video games became his primary coping mechanism during the COVID-19 pandemic. Starting from a place of helplessness and despair — alone in his apartment, uncertain about the virus, and feeling powerless against forces beyond his control — he dove into a series of games (Windbound, Subnautica, The Forest, 7 Days to Die, Valheim) where he was similarly alone and vulnerable but could actually fight back. He progressed from hating death in games to embracing it, learning to mod games to reject unfair mechanics and take control. When he received his vaccine, his obsession with the games evaporated, revealing they had been a psychological survival mechanism mirroring his real-world situation.
key_points: The early pandemic response — bogus surface transmission studies, anti-mask messaging — created a sense of total helplessness that drove Shaw into deep despair
Each survival crafting game mirrored his real situation: a person alone in a hostile world with little control, forced to gather resources and adapt to survive
Shaw's progression through the games paralleled emotional recovery — from hating death, to accepting it, to making it fun by taking control through mods
Modding games to override developer decisions became an act of reclaiming agency: 'I control this world' as a counter to pandemic helplessness
The instant loss of interest after vaccination confirmed these games were a coping mechanism, not just entertainment
money_quotes: These survival crafting games were a coping mechanism for a situation where I was helpless.
In the games I could change the situation, adapt my environment, get stronger, and beat the invisible hand that was trying to destroy me.
I even went so far as to beat the programmers of the game by installing mods to remove their decisions.
Now dying is fun because I reject the premise of the game. I take control of this world that's screwing me over with its stupid stone tools and teleporting bears trying to kill the last survivor.
It felt like a pillow had been lifted from my face and I could breathe again, which made me realize what was going on.
I'm dying, but now it's fun because I'm prepared.
Masks are like condoms. Used correctly they are 100% effective, but you won't use them correctly, so don't bother using them.
They helped me express these feelings of uselessness, and that's why I don't regret the time I spent in them.
posts.key_insight: Survival crafting games served as a psychological pressure valve during the pandemic by giving Shaw a fantasy version of his real helplessness — one where he could actually fight back, take control, and make dying fun.

posts.summary: Shaw coins the term 'Beggar Barons' to describe trillion-dollar tech companies that exploit open source developers by begging for free labor instead of paying for it. Drawing a parallel to the Robber Barons of the 1800s, he argues that companies like Apple, Microsoft, and Google built their empires on unpaid open source contributions while maintaining a facade of innovation. He illustrates this with personal anecdotes: Apple begging him to modify Mongrel for free, Microsoft expecting free documentation fixes via pull requests, and Microsoft attempting to absorb his website's traffic upon hiring him. Shaw formally defines Beggar Barons as billion-dollar companies that use open source as a facade to extract free labor they could easily afford to pay for.
key_points: Trillion-dollar tech companies systematically exploit open source developers by begging for free labor rather than paying for it, paralleling how Robber Barons used monopoly power to extract wealth
These companies maintain a facade of supporting open source and innovation while their core businesses depend on unpaid contributions they refuse to compensate
The social manipulation works through begging, pleading, and shaming — techniques like 'feel free to submit a Pull Request' shift labor costs onto individuals
Companies like Microsoft will buy GitHub for billions but won't pay the individual developers whose free labor makes their platforms valuable
Shaw carefully distinguishes Beggar Barons from small projects or companies — the defining feature is having billions of dollars yet still extracting free labor
money_quotes: We don't associate the word 'robber' with billionaires because the wealthy have had millennia to cement the association of wealth with class.
Microsoft, Apple, and Google are standing on the internet in their trillion dollar business suits with a sign that reads 'Starving and homeless. Any free labor will help.'
Once they get this free labor they rarely give credit. They're ungrateful beggars that take their donated work hours, jump in their Teslas, and ride off to make more trillions.
Microsoft thought that hiring me as an employee magically transferred all of my copyrighted material and existing products to them without requiring them to pay me at all.
The Beggar Barons will never admit that 90% of their business runs on free labor from open source.
They'll buy Github, but not Gitlab, even though Gitlab eventually made more money than Github ever will. Why? Because Gitlab is open source, so they don't see its value as a product.
You would never stand on the street and offer to buy the wallets off people who are about to donate a few dollars to you. That'd be stupid. They're giving you their money for free. Take it and run.
posts.key_insight: Trillion-dollar tech companies have replaced industrial-era robbery with a subtler exploitation: using social manipulation and open source ideology to beg developers into donating labor that these companies could trivially afford to pay for.
